target_caption: Tereré ekípo oĩva poyvi ári peteĩ korapy yvýrayty mbytépe: peteĩ térmo vakapíregui mbojegua pyre, guámpa yvyragui ojejapopyréva ha vombílla kuarepotigui.
english_caption: A tereré set placed on a cloth in the middle of a wooden patio: a thermos decorated with leather, a guampa (drinking cup) made of wood, and a metal bombilla (straw).

target_caption: Las Residentas ñemomora, 1979-pe guare, Luque ha Paraguay oja mbytépe, ha'e hína peteĩ estátua aty omomba'eguasúva kuña Paraguái py'aguasu omoĩ va'ekue ñane retã oñembo'y jey haguã.
english_caption: The monument to Las Residentas, from 1979, located between Luque and Paraguay, is a group of statues honoring the brave Paraguayan women who worked to help our nation rise again.

target_caption: Peteĩ pohã ro’ysã térã pohã ñana renda, ojeipurúva matépe ha tererépe g̃uarã, oĩva Mercado 4-pe Paraguaýpe; upépe ojekuaa Paraguái pohã ñana jepokuaa.
english_caption: A stand of cooling herbs or medicinal plants, used for mate and tereré, located at Mercado 4 in Asunción; there one can see the Paraguayan tradition of medicinal herbs.

target_caption: Peteĩ mésa ohechaukáva opaichagua Paraguái pohã ro’ysã térã pohã ñana-nguéra, ojekuaahápe ñande ypykuéra arandu pohã ñana heko oipurúva hikuái.
english_caption: A table displaying various kinds of Paraguayan cooling herbs and medicinal plants, where one can recognize the ancestral knowledge of how our forebears used these herbs.

target_caption: San Blás yvyra ra'anga, Paraguái retã rerekua, oreko irosário ijao puku morotĩ ári ha peteĩ apeao pytã; ipópe oguereko Tupã Ñe'ẽ ryru ha váculo; peteĩ tataindy itatĩ altár ykére, peteĩ mésa ao morotĩ croché enkáhegui ári.
english_caption: A wooden image of San Blas, patron saint of Paraguay, with a rosary over his long white robe and a red cape; in his hands he holds the book of God's Word and a staff; a smoking candle stands beside the altar, on a table covered with a white crochet lace cloth.

target_caption: Mbohapy óga apoha omba'apóva hína peteĩ guatarã apópe, tápia yke yváteva renondépe; ijerére oĩ ryru ha tembipuru-nguéra, peteĩ ára haku porãvápe.
english_caption: Three builders working on a path, in front of a tall adobe wall; around them are containers and tools, on a beautifully hot day.

target_caption: Peteĩ kuimba'e oguata hína tatapỹi ári; "Tata ári jehasa" niko peteĩ ritúal, peteĩ ñe'ẽ me'ẽ ha avei aguyje San Juan-pe iára guasúpe.
english_caption: A man walking over hot coals; the "walk over fire" is a ritual, a promise made and also a thanksgiving to San Juan on his feast day.

target_caption: Peteĩ "pelota tata" hendýva hína yvy ári; peteĩ ñembosarái oikóva San Juan árape, ojejapóva aokue vakapipópogui oñembohykuéva komvustíblepe, hendýva ha oñemombóva chúta hápe.
english_caption: A burning "fire ball" on the ground; a game played on San Juan's day, made from old rags soaked in fuel, set alight, and kicked around like a soccer ball.

target_caption: Peteĩ chípa, ojejapóva aramirõ ha kesúgui, oĩva kuatia jurukytyha ári, ha'e hína ñane rembi'u teete, ojehayhuvéva ha omohe’ẽva ñande reko.
english_caption: A chipa, made of cassava starch and cheese, sitting on a napkin; it is one of our most authentic foods, much loved, and one that sweetens our way of life.

target_caption: "Opygua", hérava avei "opy", ha’e Mbya Guaraníkuéra ñembo’e renda, peteĩ tenda marangatu ojejapohápe heta mba'e ijerovia rehegua, oñemombarete hag̃ua imba'eteete.
english_caption: The "opygua," also called "opy," is the prayer house of the Mbya Guaraní people, a sacred place where many religious practices are carried out to strengthen their spiritual identity.

target_caption: Sópa Paraguái niko peteĩ mbujapemi avevo ha rorã, ojejapóva avatiku'i, kesú, ryguasu rupi'a ha kambýgui; ojoguave peteĩ mbujape térã mbujape he'ẽpe ha ndaha'éi tembi'u hykue.
english_caption: Sopa Paraguaya is a fluffy, savory bread made from cornmeal, cheese, eggs, and milk; it resembles a bread or sweet bread and is not actually a soup (a liquid dish).

target_caption: Chupín ryguasu arró kesú ndive: peteĩ jukysy rãicha ojejapóva kamby ñandýguinte, ka'avo ha papandi; oje'u arró kesú teetendi, orekóva kámby ha kesú Paraguái heta porã.
english_caption: Chicken chupín with cheese rice: a stew-like dish made with cream, vegetables, and spices; it is eaten with authentic cheese rice, which has plenty of milk and Paraguayan cheese.

target_caption: Sopa ryguasu ha'eko hína pe sópa paraguái oñemyenyhẽva pe ñemongu'éva ryguasu so'o ha ka'avokuéra ndive.
english_caption: Sopa ryguasu is the Paraguayan sopa filled with shredded chicken meat and vegetables.

target_caption: Ñai'ũ apo renda Areguápe, heráva avei ñai'ũ rembiapo táva; upépe ojekuaa opaichagua mba'e, y syry jeguakuéra korapýpe g̃uarã ha ambue hembiapo porãita sa'yitáva.
english_caption: A pottery workshop in Areguá, also known as the city of clay craftsmanship; there one finds all kinds of pieces, decorative fountains for patios, and other beautiful colorful works.

target_caption: Aguakáte ijýva ojehechaukáva tape ykére. Ko yva oñemomba’eguasu ikyra porã haguére ha he’ẽ asy rupi.
english_caption: Ripe avocados shown along the roadside. This fruit is valued for its rich, creamy texture and sweet flavor.

target_caption: Peteĩ ava, Yvapovondy guataguigua, ohepyme'ẽ hína mymba ra'anga yvyra ojejapopyréva Aguapéype, tape yképe.
english_caption: An indigenous person from Yvapovondy is selling wooden animal carvings made in Aguapéy, by the roadside.

target_caption: Manduvi chyryry oñevendéva vosa'ípe tapére. Ikatu avei oje'u ku'i kósido kambýre.
english_caption: Fried peanuts being sold in small bags by the roadside. They can also be eaten ground and cooked with milk.

target_caption: Ñandutí oñembosa'y hetávagui ha oñeporãmbáva, ha'éva peteĩ mba'e porãite ñane retã megua ojejutíva pópepe.
english_caption: Ñandutí lace dyed in many colors and finely crafted, a beautiful piece from our country, sewn entirely by hand.

target_caption: Peteĩ ñandutí mba'emi ojejapóva sa'y hetávagui, tembiapojeguáva ojejapóva Paraguáipe, júpe ha pópe.
english_caption: A small piece of ñandutí lace made in many colors, a decorative craft made in Paraguay with needle and hand.

target_caption: Kurupi ra'anga, peteĩ mombe'upy herakuã'avéva Paraguáipe: Paĩ Tavyterã rembiapojeguáva yvyragui ojejapóva.
english_caption: A figure of Kurupi, one of the most well-known legends in Paraguay: a wood carving made by the Paĩ Tavyterã people.

target_caption: Jakare Yrupe (Victoria cruziana), ha’e peteĩ ñana ýpegua tuichava oĩva ysyry Paraguái ha Parana rembe’ýre. Hogue tuicháva ha apu’áva oveve yno’õ ha karugua ári; ikatu imokoĩ metros pukukue ha upévare ha’e peteĩva umi ka’avo ypegua ojekuaavéva ñane retãme.
english_caption: Jakare Yrupe (Victoria cruziana) is a large aquatic plant found along the banks of the Paraguay and Paraná rivers. Its large, round leaves float on lagoons and wetlands; they can reach up to two meters in length, making it one of the most well-known aquatic plants in our country.

target_caption: Salvia guaranitica, ojekuaáva salvia hovy térã anisádape, ha’e peteĩ ka’avo oĩva opaite árape ñane retã ha ijerére. Ijyvoty hovy téra pytãũ puku, ombojurúva eiru ha mainumbykuérape; upévare ojehayhueterei yvotytyháre iporãitéva ha oipytyvõgui tekohápe.
english_caption: Salvia guaranitica, known as blue salvia or anise sage, is a plant found year-round in our country and surrounding region. Its long blue or purple flowers attract bees and hummingbirds; this is why it is much loved by gardeners both for its beauty and for the way it supports the ecosystem.

target_caption: Jesuíta rembiapokue ymaguare ojejapóva ita ha ladríllo-gui. Ko’ã tenda ohechauka ñande rapo ha ñane retã rembiasakue.
english_caption: Old Jesuit ruins built from stone and brick. These places show our roots and the history of our nation.

target_caption: Jerokyharakuéra oikuaave’ẽva pe jeroky paraguái kaguakáre, iao ypykue. Iao po’i ha isãingo puku, omoĩ hikuái hy'a térã kambuchi iñakã ári; upépe ohechauka hikuái ikatupyry ha omoingove ñane retã reko tee.
english_caption: Dancers performing the Paraguayan bottle dance in their traditional dress. Their dresses are slim and have long skirts; they balance bottles or jars on their heads, displaying their skill and bringing our country's authentic traditions to life.

target_caption: Jerokyhára ijao ñande ypykuére, oñembojapava ñandutípe, pea ha’e Paraguái rembiapo tee. Pe ijeguaka apu’áva, ojoguáva ñandu renimbo, ha’e peteĩva umi mba’e ojekuaavéva ñane retãme ha omohe’ẽva ñande reko.
english_caption: A dancer in traditional dress, made with ñandutí lace, which is an authentic Paraguayan craft. The round adornment, which resembles a spider's web, is one of the most well-known features of our country and one that sweetens our culture.

target_caption: Tajy morotĩ avei, Paraguái yvyra mimbipa, ipotyjave oĩvo pe Costanera Encarnación-pe. Ro’y mbytépe, ijyvoty morotĩ asy ojapa pe yvyráre ha omoaig̃uivo pe arahaku oguahẽtáva; upéicha ohechauka ñane retã porãite ha ñande reko tee.
english_caption: The white tajy (lapacho), also a celebrated tree of Paraguay, in bloom along the Costanera in Encarnación. In the middle of winter, its bright white flowers cover the tree and signal the coming of warmer weather; in this way it shows the beauty of our country and our authentic way of life.

target_caption: Jopara, ñane rembi’u tee ojejapóva avati morotĩ (locro), poroto, so’o ha ka'avóetarehe. Oje’u opaite peteĩ jasypápe oñemosẽ hag̃ua Karai Octubre-pe; ha’e niko upe ikatúva ogueru ñembyahyi, upévare jajapo ko jopara ñamba’apo ha ñaneakãpu'ã hag̃ua oñondivepa.
english_caption: Jopara, our traditional dish made of white corn (locro), beans, meat, and various vegetables. It is eaten every October 1st to chase away "Karai Octubre," the spirit said to bring hunger; that is why we make this jopara, so we may work and prosper together.

target_caption: Pombero ra’ãnga, oĩva pe Museo Mitológico Ramón Elías-pe. Pombero niko pe ojekuaáva 'Pyhare Jára' ramo; ha’e peteĩ póra oñangarekóva tekoháre ha mymbakuérare, ha upéicha avei, ñande ymáguare he’iháicha, oiko ka’aguy ha okaháre.
english_caption: A figure of Pombero, on display at the Ramón Elías Mythological Museum. Pombero is known as the 'Lord of the Night'; he is a spirit who watches over the land and animals, and according to our ancestors, lives in the forests and rural areas.

target_caption: Mburukuja poty (Passiflora caerulea), ha’e peteĩ ka’avo ojupíva yvatére ha ojehecharamóva Paraguái ha ijerere, yvoty teete ramo. Ijyvoty jeguaka ñanderecha pukarãva, upévare umi misionerokuéra omoĩ hese Hesu Crísto hasy’atã hague, ha upéicha opyta ñane retã rembiapo ha tekoha rechaukaha ramo.
english_caption: The mburukuja flower (Passiflora caerulea), or passionflower, is a climbing vine admired in Paraguay and the surrounding region as a true flower. Its striking, eye-catching bloom led missionaries to associate it with the Passion of Jesus Christ, and so it has remained as a symbol of our nation's heritage and natural environment.

target_caption: Mainumby hovyũ, ha’e peteĩ guyra’i oĩva Paraguáipe ha ojehayhueterei ñande reko guaraníme. Oguahẽvo ñande rógape, he’ise outaha mba’e porã, vy’a ha py’aguapy; ha’e niko teko porã rechaukaha ha ñandembojoaju ñande yvy ndive.
english_caption: The dark blue hummingbird is a small bird found in Paraguay and deeply loved in our Guaraní culture. When it visits our home, it is said to bring good things, joy, and peace; it is a symbol of well-being and connects us with our land.

target_caption: "Panteón de los Héroes": ko’ápe opytu’u ñande héroes, umi oñorairõ ha odefendeva’ekue ñane retã Paraguái, ha upeichaite ojapo hikuái ñande historia.
english_caption: The "Pantheon of Heroes": here rest our heroes, those who fought and defended our nation of Paraguay, and in doing so shaped our history.

target_caption: Ko'ápe ojekuaa peteĩ ta'anga ojejapóva yvyrágui, ha'éva peteĩ ava guaraní rova ha rete, hesu'úva py'aguasu ha iñakãrague hũ.
english_caption: Here we see a sculpture made of wood, depicting the face and body of a Guaraní man, with a brave expression and dark hair.

target_caption: Peteĩ oga'i porãite ojejapóva yvyra ha tujúgui, ha ombohogahoja kapi'igui omo'ãva chupe kuarahy ha amandáugui.
english_caption: A beautiful little house made of wood and adobe, with a thatched grass roof that shelters it from the sun and rain.

target_caption: Ava guarani kuéra oñembyaty kirirîháme tataypýpe ha oñembo'ehaguã oñondivepa, upéicharo omombarete jopyhu ha jerovia.
english_caption: Guaraní indigenous people gather quietly around the fire to pray together, and in this way strengthen their bonds and faith.

target_caption: Ava guaraní mitãrusukuéra ojeroky hína tataypy jere rupi, oñangarekóvo hikuái imba’e teére ha ohechaukávo mba’éichapa oiko peteĩ ñe’ẽme joaju ha vy’ápe tavayguakuéra ma'ême.
english_caption: Guaraní indigenous youth dance around the fire, caring for their traditions and showing how the community lives together in unity, language, and joy.

target_caption: Guampa ojejapóva yvyra okáigui ha oguereko mba'erechaukaha vakapi gui ha bombilla (takuapi) ojejapo kuarepotigui.
english_caption: A guampa made from a hollowed wood log, decorated with a leather emblem, accompanied by a bombilla (drinking straw) made of metal.

target_caption: Ko’ápe jahecha peteĩ reviro haku porãva, ojejapóva aramirõ ha ñandýgui paila ári; omoirũ chupe peteĩ so’o mbusu hovyũ asýva ha tupi’a mbojy, ha’éva tembi’u omombaretepáva ñande rete.
english_caption: Here we see a steaming hot reviro, made of toasted flour and lard in a pan; it is served with a dark, rich beef stew and a boiled egg, a meal that nourishes the body.

target_caption: Guataha kuéra oñembyaty jerovia ha jerovia guasúpe, Tupao Ka'akupe renondépe, oguahe hikuái mombyryguive ohepyme'ẽ haguã ipromésa ha oaguyjeveme'ẽ haguã Tupãsy de los Milagros-pe.
english_caption: Pilgrims gather in faith and great devotion in front of the Basilica of Caacupé, having traveled from afar to fulfill their promises and to give thanks to the Virgin of Miracles.

target_caption: Peteî ajaka henyhe "chipa argolla"gui, tembi'u ojehayhuvéva ñane retã Paraguáipe, oînte va'erã jehomombyrýpe ha aty guasu rogayguákuéra ndive, akói hyakuãvurei hese kesu ha aramirõ.
english_caption: A basket full of "chipa argolla," a beloved food in our country of Paraguay, always present at long trips and large family gatherings, always carrying the wonderful aroma of cheese and cassava starch.

target_caption: Ñane tatakua tee ojejapóva tuju ha ladríllogui; upépe jahesy ñande chipa ha opaichagua tembi'u he'ẽ asýva, ombopupu ha omohyakuã porãva jepe ñande rogapy.
english_caption: Our traditional clay oven made of mud and brick; in it we bake our chipa and all kinds of sweet foods, which boil and fill our home with wonderful aromas.

target_caption: Peteĩ kambuchi oñemohendáva apyte ao ári, ha'éva pe akã rupa ojejapóva ao vírogui ikatu haguãicha kuñanguéra ogueraha y iñakã ári oñemyangue'u'ỹre.
english_caption: A clay jar resting on a head pad — a coiled cloth ring made of fabric — that allows women to carry water on their heads without spilling it.

target_caption: Urutau kakuaa oñemombo’y yvyra rakãre ha’ete hína yvyra parakuéicha, oñangarekóvo imemby michĩmi rehe.
english_caption: A large urutau bird perched on a tree branch, looking just like a piece of wood, watching over its small chick.

target_caption: Jasy Jatere, pe mita'i akã sa'yju oikóva ka'aguýre asaje pyte jave; oguerekóva ipópe ivara ju omoasẽ haguã mitãme ha ogueraha haguã chupekuéra hendive.
english_caption: Jasy Jatere, the blond-haired child who roams the forest at midday; he carries a golden staff in his hand, used to lure children away and take them with him.

target_caption: Ko’ápe ojekuaa porã pe Chipa guasu he’ẽ asýva, ojejapóva avati kyre’ỹ ha kesu paraguáigui; ijyvate ha overa asy hína, ha’éva tembi’u opave’ỹva’erã ñande meságui.
english_caption: Here we clearly see the deliciously sweet chipa guasu, made from fresh corn and Paraguayan cheese; it is tall and golden, a dish that should never be missing from our table.

target_caption: Kuña ha mitãkuña oiporúva ao guaraní teete. Ko’ã ao ohechauka yvypóra ypykue rekotee ha ñande rapo guaraní ymaite guive.
english_caption: A woman and a girl wearing authentic Guaraní clothing. These garments display the traditions of our ancestral people and our deep Guaraní roots.

target_caption: Trampa yvyrágui ojejapóva okaháre, ojeipurúva ojejapyhy hag̃ua mymba michĩva.
english_caption: A wooden trap built outdoors, used to catch small animals.

target_caption: Alonsito oguapýva hupa ári, ojejapóva tuju memégui ha ojogua tatakua. Ko guyra niko ojapo hupa yvyra térã yvyramáta rehe.
english_caption: An Alonsito (rufous hornero) sitting on its nest, made entirely of mud and shaped like a clay oven. This bird builds its nest on tree branches or fence posts.

target_caption: Óga okaháre ojejapóva yvyrágui ha hi'ári kapi'i. Ko'ã ógape ojeipuru opa mba'e oĩva upe rupi.
english_caption: A rural house built from wood with a thatched grass roof on top. In these homes, everything used is sourced from the surrounding area.

target_caption: Vori vori, tembi'u paraguái teete ojejapóva ryguasu rykuégui, avati ku'i ha kesú, ha'e hína peteĩ tembi'u ojeikuaavéva Paraguáipe.
english_caption: Vori vori, an authentic Paraguayan dish made from chicken broth, cornmeal, and cheese; it is one of the most well-known dishes in Paraguay.

target_caption: Mandi'o pyahu, rapo oñekotevẽva Paraguáipe; oje'u oñembopupúva térã oñembochyryrýva.
english_caption: Fresh cassava, a root crop essential to Paraguay; it is eaten boiled or fried.
